sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Mr. Jackson, a 45-year-old male, presents with a 3-week history of right elbow pain, specifically on the outside part of the elbow, which worsens with movement and improves with rest.
The pain is described as sharp, stabbing, aching, and throbbing, and is rated an 8/10 with activity and 3/10 at rest.
The pain is not radiating to the arm or shoulder.
He has been experiencing the pain despite taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, which only provide temporary relief.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of type 2 diabetes, mild intermittent asthma, and a past surgical history of gallbladder removal 10 years ago.
He has an allergy to penicillin.
He is taking metformin 500mg twice a day and has been taking flovent twice a day and albuterol as needed for asthma.
Review of systems: None mentioned.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes
Flovent twice a day for asthma
Albuterol as needed for asthma
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
Moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow
Pain with passive range of motion of the right elbow
Mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist
Normal unremarkable exam otherwise
Test Results:
Right elbow x-ray: No acute fracture or other bony abnormality, no malalignment or joint effusion.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse from increased tennis activity.
Problem list:
Tennis elbow; Diabetes type 2; Asthma (mild intermittent); Allergy to penicillin; History of gallbladder removal.
Plan:
I recommended that Mr. Jackson rest his right arm as much as possible, stop tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoid repetitive stress in that arm.
I also advised him to ice the right elbow for 20 minutes, 3 times a day to reduce pain and inflammation.
I prescribed Ultram 50mg once a day for pain relief.
I will provide a brace for his right forearm to help with discomfort.
I will see him in 4 weeks to reevaluate his elbow and consider additional imaging or physical therapy if needed.
Follow-up:
I will see Mr. Jackson in 4 weeks to reevaluate his elbow.
